Generative artificial intelligence for enzyme design: Recent advances in models and applications
================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Shuixiu Wen 1 a, Wen Zheng 2 a, Uwe T.Bornscheuer 3, Shuke Wu 1

Show more

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cogsc.2025.101010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cogsc.2025.101010 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2452223625000148&orderBeanReset=true)

Enzyme catalysis is a key enabling technology for green and sustainable production of chemicals. Developing suitable enzymes is at the heart of this technology, which is currently changing by Artificial Intelligence (AI) such as machine learning. AI-based methods were used for enzyme discovery and design. We review the recent advances in generative AI models for enzyme design, with a particular focus on those that have been validated by experiments. Furthermore, we discuss the applications of the enzymes designed by generative AI, including artificial luciferases, non-heme iron (II)-dependent oxygenases, and P450 enzymes. We provide our opinions on several current issues encountered in computational enzyme design. With the fast development of new generative models in enzymes and the implementation of these models by the research community, we believe that the precise design of efficient enzymes with new catalytic functions and/or potential industrial applications will be a mature method in the near future.

Introduction
------------

Enzymes are the “core chips” of biological production or conversion of chemicals in a green and sustainable manner via single- or multi-step enzyme catalysis [1∗, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], as well as cell-based metabolic engineering and synthetic biology [7, 8, 9, 10]. To transfer natural enzymes into industrial biocatalysts, a widely used key enabling technology is directed evolution, a mainly experimental approach, by applying mutation-screening cycles on the protein level (_i.e._, molecular breeding) without a deep understanding of the molecular mechanism [11, 12, 13]. Although many successes for the engineering of enzymes have been reported by academia and industry, directed evolution is still time-, labor-, equipment- and resource-consuming, often limited to natural enzymes as starting templates, frequently trapped in some local optimum of evolution path, and it is challenging to create new functions. In the last decade, due to the advances in catalytic mechanisms and physics-based simulations, semi-rational or even rational approaches have been developed and applied for engineering or redesign of improved enzyme variants [14∗, 15, 16, 17], yet this is still largely limited to natural reactions and natural sequences as the prototypes.

Recently, breakthroughs in artificial intelligence together with rapid accumulation of big data of protein sequences and structures provide a unique data-driven approach for enzyme discovery, engineering, and design [18, 19, 20, 21, 22]. This is obvious from the Nobel prize in Chemistry awarded in 2024 to David Baker for his achievements in computational protein design and to Demis Hassabis and John Jumper for protein structure prediction using AlphaFold [23]. These tools are very important for machine-learning assisted data-driven enzyme engineering, which has been developed to improve the stability, activity, and selectivity of many industrially used enzymes (_e.g._, PET hydrolases [24,25], imine reductases [26], and amine transaminases [27]) which had been intensively reviewed very recently [28∗∗, 29, 30]. Besides the prediction of mutations, AI-based methods could also generate new sequences for protein design [31, 32, 33∗]. Herein, we focus our discussions on recent developments of AI-based generative models for the creation of novel enzymes (instead of simple variants of existing enzymes) for production of chemicals (Fig.1).

Creating custom enzymes is a grand challenge in protein science. Our latest research, appearing today in [Science](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu2454) and recently across multiple preprints [[1](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.09.25.614868),[2](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.11.13.623507)], showcases how AI-driven protein design can now be used to generate efficient protein catalysts with complex active sites tailored for specific chemical reactions.

What is enzyme design?
----------------------

Enzymes are proteins that accelerate chemical reactions under mild conditions, far outperforming what even the best human chemists can achieve. Found in all living cells, some natural enzymes have been repurposed for biofuel and pharmaceutical production. They are even present in most laundry detergents, where they chew apart oil stains. Billions of dollars worth of industrial enzymes are produced and sold globally each year.

Generating custom enzymes that speed up important chemical reactions—whether building up pharmaceutical compounds or breaking down microplastics—would allow us to harness nature’s efficiency without relying on harsh solvents or fossil fuels. However, traditional enzyme engineering has relied on modifying existing proteins rather than building them from scratch.

“Traditional enzyme design is like buying a suit from a thrift store: the fit will probably be a little off. With AI, we can now tailor-make enzymes to ensure a perfect fit for every step of the reaction,” explained Sam Pellock, a co-lead author of the Science study and acting instructor in the lab.

Building new serine hydrolases
------------------------------

Sam, Anna, and Kiera led a project focused on accelerating a specific chemical reaction: breaking ester bonds. The enzymes they designed, called serine hydrolases, are unlike any found in nature. Through in silico modeling and lab validation, the team confirmed that these enzymes effectively bind and cleave ester compounds as intended.

![Image 5](https://www.bakerlab.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/AI-enzyme-BY-Sam-Pellock-University-of-Washington-web-1024x576.jpg)

This computationally designed serine hydrolase accelerates a multistep chemical reaction.

“We tested our AI-designed enzymes in the lab and found they can be quite efficient,” said Anna Lauko, a co-lead author and recent PhD graduate. “There is still room for improvement because these chemical transformations are complex, but I’m thrilled by what we can now accomplish with the latest generation of design tools.”

Over 300 computer-generated proteins were tested in the lab. Of these, a subset showed reactivity with chemical probes, indicating successful installation of an activated catalytic serine. Through iterative rounds of design and screening, the team ultimately identified several highly efficient catalysts, including ones with activity levels that far exceed prior computationally designed esterases. Structural analysis revealed that the designed enzymes closely matched their intended architectures, with crystal structures deviating by less than 1 Å from their computational models.

These results highlight the efficiency of integrating deep learning-based protein design with a novel assessment tool to evaluate catalytic preorganization across multiple reaction states.

A greener future
----------------

This work builds on other recent AI breakthroughs in our lab, including the development of a deep learning tool for [modeling protein-small molecule conformational ensembles](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.09.25.614868). This tool was used to create retroaldolase enzymes with considerably higher catalytic efficiencies than any pre-deep learning design for this reaction, as well [novel enzymes that harness metal ions for catalysis](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.11.13.623507) with orders of magnitude higher catalytic efficiencies than previously designed metallohydrolases. This work is rapidly expanding the possibilities of enzyme design.

Kiera Sumida, a co-lead author of the Science study and a PhD trainee, is already applying these methods to tackle plastic degradation, demonstrating the broad potential of this approach. “I believe we’re not far from having custom enzymes that will help create a greener economy,” she said.

This work was made possible through support from the US National Institutes of Health, Defense Threat Reduction Agency, Howard Hughes Medical Institute, Washington Research Foundation, Open Philanthropy, The Audacious Project, Microsoft, and Schmidt Futures, with experimental support from the US Department of Energy.

# Breaking Natureâs Limits: Enzyme Design for a Sustainable Future Powered by Intelligent Architectureâ¢

The growing demand for new enzymes is catalyzing the evolution of industrial biotechnology, biopharmaceuticals, bioremediation, and R&D while simultaneously driving the transformation to sustainable manufacturing processes.

As the worldâs population continues to grow, there is an urgent need for enzyme-based solutions that can redefine manufacturing and provide healthy, nutritious food more efficiently with lower environmental impact. These demands have spurred researchers and companies to push the boundaries beyond what was once thought possible with enzyme engineering.

â

### Reimagining Enzyme Design

Until recently, the strategy for developing industrial enzymes relied on identifying those found in nature and enhancing them via incremental improvements through protein engineering. This approach, however, results in âFrankensteinâ enzymes that are not always suited for the demands of industrial processes.

To transform industries and meet global sustainability goals, we must transcend the limits of working with natural enzymes alone and redesign the fundamental structures and functions of enzymes, rewriting natureâs catalytic tools for yet-to-be-imagined applications.

Biomatter has reimagined enzyme design in response to these complexities, taking a radically different approach: starting with the ideal end state in mind. That means defining clear objectives, requirements, constraints, and acceptance criteria. It means designing enzymes that exhibit precise function and performance characteristicsâthe specific chemistries and functions tailored to the specific applications. This shift from identifying and modifying to a *de novo*, ground-up design approach marks a pivotal change in biotechnological innovation.

â

### Pioneering Generative AI Enzyme Design

Starting in 2019, [Biomatter](https://biomatter.ai/) pioneered the use of generative AI to expand the protein sequence space by developing [ProteinGAN](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/789719v2), a specialized variant of the generative adversarial network that can learn natural protein sequence diversity and enable the generation of functional protein sequences.

Since then, Biomatter has perfected proprietary generative AI tools and physics engines to design enzymes from the ground up, moving beyond the constraints of nature's blueprints.

An example of Biomatterâs enzyme engineering and precision fermentation prowess is its recent collaboration with Kirin Holdings to increase the production of [Lacto-N-fucopentaose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1096717623001829?via%3Dihub) (LNFP I), the second most abundant fucosylated human milk oligosaccharide (HMO) in breast milk after 2'-Fucosyllactose (2'-FL). Until now, producing LNFP I at a high titer without producing 2'-FL has been a challenge.

LNFP I is produced through an enzymatic reaction catalyzed by the enzyme Î±1,2-fucosyltransferase (Î±1,2-FucT).

Biomatter redesigned the substrate binding pocket of Î±1,2-fucosyltransferase (FsFucT) and the hydrophobic residues on the enzyme's surface. The resulting enzyme selectively produced LNFP I compared to 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL) and was optimized for batch-fed fermentation.

As a result of this work, the strain expressing the newly redesigned FsFucT enzyme showed increased accumulation of LNFP I and only produced a trace amount of 2'-FL. The ability to decrease the amount of by-products is essential when manufacturing on an industrial scale.

While embracing AI for enzyme design offers unprecedented potential, it also introduces certain perceived risks and limitations that merit consideration. These include:

It is important to recognize these considerations not as insurmountable obstacles but as integral aspects of the current innovation landscape in synthetic biology. These challenges can be overcome by supporting AI with physics and empirical scientific inquiry to refine and validate designs.

Biomatter uses fully experimentally validated AI and physics-based software tools to address the above challenges. It showcases that designed enzymes can be developed, tested, and produced quickly. Our methodology erases the boundaries between the conceivable and the achievable, the possible and the impossible.

Biomatter's enzyme design capabilities enable near-perfect control over enzyme design, making previously impossible technical jumps possible while opening the door to unique enzyme-based products and technologies. The company develops tailor-made enzymes with unique functions that are fully optimized for industrial applications by increasing activity, specificity, stability, and expression levels by functionalizing nonfunctional scaffolds and discovering new proteins on accelerated timelines.

We are on the cusp of revolutionizing protein engineering, entering an era where researchers are not constrained by nature's initial designs but can create bespoke, fit-for-purpose enzymes.

SUMMARY:
Biotechnology industries have been evolved by adapting AI technologies in enzyme designs for specific uses in biofuels, pharmaceuticals and food sector.

Seton Hall University Seton Hall University eRepository @ Seton Hall eRepository @ Seton Hall Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annals of Student Reviews Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry 2025 Artificial Intelligence in Enzyme Engineering Artificial Intelligence in Enzyme Engineering Margret Pagare Follow this and additional works at: https://scholarship.shu.edu/student-reviews Part of the Chemistry Commons Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 21 Artificial Intelligence in Enzyme Engineering Author: Margret Pagare Editor/Reviewer: Dr. Gregory Wiedman Chief Editor: Dr. Gregory Wiedman Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 22 Introduction Enzyme engineering is the modification of enzymes' amino acid sequences to produce novel biocatalysts with enhanced properties.1 Enzyme engineering has yielded a variety of designs in enzymes for medicine, pharmaceuticals, food production, and biosensing.2 The purpose of enzyme engineering includes substrate specificity, enantioselectivity, thermodynamic stability, stability in cosolvents, expressibility, and solubility.3 The two strategies of enzyme engineering include rational design and directed evolution.4,5 Direct evolution uses mutagenesis for iterative production of mutant libraries followed by screening for the desired properties in the enzyme variants.5 Rational design requires structural analysis and comprehensive computational modeling of enzymes, considering that all properties of amino acids and mimicking their interaction with the environment.2,4 Both strategies work well hand in hand; however, they still result in a lot of work and effort for each case of a biocatalyst optimization. Artificial intelligence is a significant division of computer science that focuses on using hardware and software to imitate human intelligence and processes, such as learning and problem-solving. This type of artificial intelligence is referred to as machine learning. Its application in various fields such as enzyme engineering and Biochemistry.6 Research in enzyme design and AI integration have resulted in remarkable advancements which have completely changed the field. In the early stages AI primarily focused on bioinformatics. Machine learning (ML) was used to assess large genomic and proteomic datasets and this established the foundation for cutting edge AI applications in protein engineering.7 AI is increasingly applied in enzyme engineering, metabolic pathway analysis, and predicting detailed biochemical reactions.8 The synergy of AI and high-throughput screening has revolutionized drug discovery by significantly cutting down on time and expenses.9 Machine Learning (ML) has been gaining attention in the field of biocatalyst design. A data-based approach has been used to identify trends in existing data and consequently predict the properties of enzymes of previously unencountered but comparable input. ML-based design can generate new and promising variants based on the data collected. Various machine learning (ML) algorithms have been utilized in enzyme engineering.11 For instance, random forests have been used to predict protein solubility, support vector machines and decision trees to predict how mutations affect enzyme stability, K-nearest-neighbor classifiers to predict enzyme function and mechanisms, and different scoring and clustering algorithms for the quick annotation of functional sequences.12 Machine learning methods have been widely employed in finding trends in data helping predict protein structures, improve enzyme stability, solubility, and function.2 The success of the ML predictor depends on the quality of the data set used for training and the algorithm's efficiency. This paper highlights the advances made in implementing artificial intelligence in enzyme engineering. Within this context, we discuss the types of AI, ML, and deep learning. Furthermore, we look at data collection methods and how they are applied to enzyme engineering. We also discuss the weaknesses and challenges faced while implementing AI in enzyme design.2 Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 23 1. Fundamental Principle of Artificial Intelligence 1.1 Types of Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence allows computers to simulate cognitive processes. These abilities encompass a wide range of computer programs that perform tasks like human cognition, for instance, learning vision and logical reasoning. The three main types of artificial intelligence include narrow, general, and super intelligence. Narrow intelligence is the predominant form of AI designed for a single task, like Siri, Alexa, or virtual assistants.6 Narrow AI is also used to process a large amount of data. However, weak AI has limitations. It cannot think for itself, learn from new situations like humans do, or handle tasks requiring creativity or intuition. However, it is built to be efficient and fast for its specific purpose, helping companies stay competitive through automation. General intelligence, the future of AI, could think and function like humans, perform tasks like vision, language processing, general thinking, and be broad and adaptable. The major drawback is the ability to replicate transfer learning and common sense. Research on enhancing GI continues, and potential advancement could lead to significant technological and scientific progress.13 Super intelligence is the apex of AI. It outperforms any form of intelligence, whether human or artificial, by continuously learning from itself, leading to an exponential increase in intelligence. While this self-improvement could bring about a technological revolution, it also presents ethical and technical dangers. The importance of ML algorithms is to find patterns in the available data. This data usually consists of data points with several features, such as enzyme features, properties of amino acids, and secondary and tertiary structure. 1.2 Machine Learning Algorithms Machine Learning is a subset of AI where algorithms are used to make predictions based on data. There are two types of machine learning supervised and unsupervised. In unsupervised high dimensional data is compressed into a lower number of dimensions or find data clusters. Supervised learning, one or more properties such as enzyme activity are designed as labels and the goal is to engineer a predictor that will return labels for unseen data points based on their descriptors using a labeled training data set as shown in Figure 1.2 The workflow of supervised ML algorithms has three steps: Step1: Data collection and feeding into the ML algorithm. Step 2: the data is split into training and test subsets. The training set is used to fine tune parameters of an ML based predictor. Step 3:The test subset is used for the final evaluation.2 Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 24 Figure 1: Workflow of a Supervised machine learning algorithms including the challenges encountered at each step. Step 1 involves experimentation, collecting data and feeding the data Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 25 into a computer system. Step 2 the data previously obtained is split into training and test subset for future use. Step 3 the test subset is used for the final evaluation.2 1.3 Deep Learning Deep Learning refers to specialized learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple layers to model complex, large patterns in large datasets. Deep learning has impacted biochemistry, natural language processing, and image recognition.7 2. Databases Relevant to Enzyme Engineering 2.1 Data Collection Process Machine learning algorithms heavily rely on previously stored data in databases. The most used databases in enzyme engineering include protein sequence databases, followed by databases for protein structures. There are also databases for protein stability, solubility, or catalytic properties. Figure 2 illustrates some various advantages and disadvantages of given databases. Figure 2: Various protein databases used in enzyme engineering arranged by properties , size, advantages and disadvantages.2 Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 26 2.2 Challenges Encountered Collecting Data Due to resource and time constraints, individual experiments often yield small datasets, numbering only in the dozens to hundreds of variants. This limited data quantity from single experiments is insufficient for robust ML training. Combining data from different experiments is problematic because data collection protocols and reporting standards vary between research teams, making it difficult to pool data reliably.10 The lack of robust data analysis protocols (e.g., for curve fitting) raises concerns about the accuracy of reported values like melting temperatures or kinetic rate constants. Advancements in experimental methods can render older databases obsolete, especially if they are no longer actively curated. Inconsistent reporting guidelines lead to missing or erroneous values for important databases descriptors.2 Existing databases were not designed for ML applications, often requiring data tidying to be usable. Many databases do not adhere to the fair principles (findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable), hindering automated data discovery and machine utilization. The selection of protein variants for testing is often skewed towards anticipated positive results, and negative results are frequently unreported, leading to non-uniform sampling of the parameter space and biased ML predictor performance. Manual curation, while helpful, cannot eliminate issues like misannotations of protein functions and the propagation of errors from previously disproven findings. Many existing databases lack consistent and ongoing quality control and error correction procedures.13 2.3 New Methods for High-throughput Data Collection Data collection methods include next-generation sequencing (NGS), liquid handling robotics, Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), deep mutational scanning (DMS), and droplet-based microfluidics. NGS is a new way to scan DNA and RNA. This has given scientists much basic information about how living things work. Because it's so fast, we can now read the entire DNA code of a human in just one day! This speed has made research possible that we couldn't even imagine before, and it's being used everywhere in biology and medicine. While the first versions of this technology were already fast and accurate, newer, longer-reading versions are even better at looking at tricky parts of DNA.2 Liquid-handling robotics, combined with miniaturized reaction chambers like well plates (up to 1536 wells) and even smaller microcapillaries (potentially hundreds of thousands per plate), is standard for high-throughput screening of enzyme reactions. While these methods offer significant parallelization, the number of enzyme variants analyzed typically doesn't exceed one million. Screening must move from solid supports like wells and capillaries to fluid-based systems to explore even larger numbers of variants. FACS enables the screening of up to 108 variants of enzymes. It requires fluorescent substrate to be confined within or on the cell's exterior to connect genotype and phenotype. Another approach involves sorting enzymes with their corresponding DNA and a fluorescent substrate within hydrogel beads.2 Droplet-based microfluidics is a powerful technology that can quickly create huge numbers (up to 100 million) of identical tiny droplets on a chip. It also allows for complex manipulations of these Seton Hall Biochemistry II Annal of Student Reviews Vol 3. No. 3 2025 27 droplets, like merging, incubating, mixing, splitting, and sorting. This method combines the flexibility of standard lab plate screening with the high speed of cell sorting techniques. Because the droplets are so small, the cost of screening each sample is drastically reduced compared to using automated lab plates. This ultra-fast screening in these tiny water-in-oil droplets has become a new way to find even infrequent occurrences within extensive collections of samples (millions to hundreds of millions) at a low cost. DMS can connect the genotype and phenotype without intensive protein purification or characterization processes. Deep mutational scanning (DMS) is a fast and easy way to determine how a protein's sequence affects its stability and function. It involves creating many versions of a gene, seeing which versions produce the desired traits, and then comparing the gene sequences before and after selection to measure how well each version performed. DMS has also been used to help determine how proteins fold into their 3D shape by identifying pairs of gene positions that strongly influence each other and are likely close together in the protein's structure. However, there are still some experimental and computational hurdles to overcome before DMS can be widely used to determine the structure of larger proteins.10 3. Artificial Application to Enzyme Engineering 3.1 Enzyme Engineering Enzyme engineering is a process where enzymes are designed and modified to improve their properties or function. Techniques from biochemistry, microbiology, and protein chemistry alter the structure of enzymes, improving stability, specificity, or catalytic efficiency for medical, industrial, and environmental applications. Enzymes have a wide range of uses, which include drug development, biofuel production, and the food industry. Enzyme AI models are used in enzyme design to optimize catalytic properties and increase stability.7 Ryu et al demonstrated using neural networks to predict enzyme commission EC numbers ( enzyme commission refers to an international committee that classifies enzymes based on their reactions), paving the way for more efficient enzyme engineering in industrial biotechnology.14 Generative AI was introduced by Wie and Warshel in-order to resolve the challenges in understanding and engineering enzyme due to the intricate relationship between their sequence and function. They explore the use of AI in predicting the impact of mutations on enzyme properties, providing a rationale for enzyme evolution, and interpreting protein sequence information. # Hands-on perspectives on AI-driven innovation in industrial biotech applications

## How can AI be applied to industrial biotechnology and is it driving innovation?

Participants in an IWBio workshop at BRAIN Biotech headquarters in Zwingenberg discussed how AI can be used in industrial biotechnology and if it drives innovation. Below are the answers.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a hot topic - so in industrial biotechnology, but how can AI be implemented and does it drive innovation? These questions were selected by members of the industrial biotechnology association [IWBio e.V.](https://www.iwbio.de) during a workshop on April 1st, 2025 hosted by board member *Martin Langer*  (BRAIN Biotech AG). Together with board members the event was co-organized by the IWBio members *Dr. Anja Schwenzfeier* (AB Enzymes) and *Peter Kitschmann* (Bausch & Ströbel). Perspectives on the implementation and impact of AI along the innovation value chain - from contract research and manufacturing organizations to legal advice in this field - were discussed.

![]()

AI provides correlations where human mechanistic thinking ends.

Klaus Mauch  - Co-Founder, Former Managing Director Yokogawa Insilico Biotechnology GmbH

*Klaus Mauch* set the scene during a keynote vividly explaining computing power and data sizes handled by today’s AI tools and defining AI as a provider of correlations where the human mechanistic world ends. He shared advantages and disadvantages of using cloud-based systems or in-house solutions for data management as well as potential costs for implementation of AI in daily business. Implementation of AI can be challenging due to complexity of the technology, lacking expertise of employees, and sometimes the missing explainability of results. The critical amount and quality of data used to train AI was a challenge echoed by the workshop participants.

Mauch impressively demonstrated that AI is not just a trend but is already present in our daily lives. Its use will speed up and optimize biotechnological R&D as well as production processes leading to a market advantage for companies considering the implementation as a strategic topic.

### key insights from the workshop speakers

#### **AI tools that bypass patents and set new benchmarks**

*Dr. Ingmar Schuster* (Exazyme GmbH) shared his experience with tools for scientific literature searches & proprietary developments for novel protein engineering. Current benchmarks outperforming human performance have been shown for using free (e.g. PaperQA2) and paid versions (e.g. Elicit) of AI tools for scientific review writing - making literature search much easier. He showcased own developed tools and combination of tools that help design novel enzymes beyond directed evolution and rational design methods. Minimizing the risk of infringement of existing patents (less than 80% sequence similarity) was achieved by own developed inverse folding tools. Open-source options are RFDiffusion and Protein MPNN.

## 

![Man standing in front of an auditorium, talking. ]()

#### **"Combine wisely - Leverage the potential of** AI through integration!”

How BRAIN Biotech implemented AI in enzyme development processes was shared by *Dr. Simon Peter Godehard*. He presented BRAIN Biotech’s technology platform MetXtra™ for identification of tailored enzymes.

After vividly explaining the number of possible combinations in enzyme sequence space as a starry sky for a 200 amino acid enzyme (20^200) - He underlined the importance of downsizing the data set, e.g. by predictive modeling. By integrating AI with sequence and structural bioinformatics, enzyme developments have been accelerated, reducing timelines and efforts in the lab. Next to the use of prediction tools, there are still applications for functional screening and rational bioprospecting of enzymes: e.g. if no biocatalyst is known for the intended conversion.

Next to the use of prediction tools, there are still applications for functional screening and rational bioprospecting of enzymes: e.g. if no biocatalyst is known for the intended conversion.

#### **Virtual fermentation platform: design, process and maintenance**

*Dr. Martin Kessler* (Wacker Chemie AG) elaborated on three potential use cases for AI support currently under investigation with partners.

#### **AI in manufacturing – finding the best recipe**

How data can be provided by real experiments and CFD simulation to train AI for filling optimization was shown by *Maximilian Burger* (Bausch & Ströbel). Unwanted air bubble inclusion during the injection of a liquid in a vial was used as a readout to generate CFD data and real data about the quality of the filling process.  Together with machine learning using ensemble neuronal networks the optimal parameter for the liquid filling process – the recipe – shall be predicted in future.

#### **IP regulation of AI products**

*Dr. Florian Reiling* elaborated on the patentability of AI systems and tools in general, as well as the protectability of AI-generated results and processes. While AI systems and tools may be eligible for (software) patent protection, such protection is likely only available in very specific cases. Conversely, AI-generated results and processes are rather difficult to protect, as both copyright and patent protection necessitate human contribution, which is typically absent in the case of AI-generated works (however, trade secret protection may be a feasible option).

Participants of the workshop were made aware of the importance of carefully selecting training data and its sources. Risks may arise from external and/or internal data which (i) is subject to third-party rights and/or (ii) faces other chain-of-title issues. Dr. Reiling suggested that adapted license management and employee training could serve as measures to minimise these risks.

Dr. Reiling also emphasised that European AI regulation was adopted in August and 2024 and has entered in force in February 2025. Implementation will be carried out through a staggered process until 2030. Monitoring compliance with these obligations is fundamental in reducing related risks.

### Where implementation of AI in biotechnology still falls short

While AI’s potential is undeniable, the participants also shed light on critical challenges that must be addressed before it becomes a seamless part of biotechnological workflows:

### CONCLUSION:

During the IWBio workshop, it was confirmed that the use of AI in combination with machine learning is already a reality along the industrial biotechnology value chain. It also became clear that data quality, regulatory clarity and professional integration into existing processes are essential for efficient implementation. The workshop participants agreed that companies that use AI strategically will have a market advantage in the future - also in terms of their innovations.
